9	# Technical Design: Bead State Machine and Naming-Role Decisions
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This TD is the canonical reference for what bead state means in DDx and where
14	each observed name in the codebase belongs. It exists because schema, docs,
15	code, and persisted data have drifted: the bead-record JSON Schema enumerates
16	six statuses (the bd/br canonical set), FEAT-004 still documents three, and
17	several additional names appear in code paths (`done`, `needs_human`,
18	`pending`, `ready`, `review`, `needs_investigation`) without a single decision
19	record explaining whether each is a status, a derived queue category, an
20	event kind, a label, or worker state.
21	
22	The five in-flight hygiene beads each introduce new state-machine vocabulary:
23	
24	- ddx-b24e9630 — `no_changes_*` outcome verification (NoChangesContract)
25	- ddx-3c154349 — auto-triage of stuck beads (TriageContract)
26	- ddx-aede917d — drain pause on quota exhaustion (QuotaPauseContract)
27	- ddx-c6e3db02 — rate-limit retry behavior (RateLimitRetryContract)
28	- ddx-da11a34a — store-lock contention handling (LockContentionContract)
29	
30	Without one TD that nails down the categories and the transition matrix,
31	those five beads will each independently invent overlapping vocabulary.
32	
33	This TD authors the design only. The mechanical reconciliation
34	(schema-vs-FEAT-004 alignment, code rename of non-status names, CI guards,
35	hygiene-bead AC substitution, migration survey) is filed as sibling beads.
36	
37	## Critical Constraint (per ADR-004)
38	
39	The bead-record envelope must remain compatible with the bd/br interchange
40	contract. The persisted `status` enum is **fixed** at the bd/br canonical six
41	values:
42	
43	```
44	open, in_progress, closed, blocked, proposed, cancelled
45	```
46	
47	DDx-specific execution semantics live in **labels**, **events**, or the
48	preserved-extras **Extra** map — never in new statuses. Adding a new persisted
49	status requires upstream bd/br coordination plus an ADR-004 amendment. This
50	TD does not authorize any such addition.
51	
52	## Today's Observed State (verified)
53	
54	- `cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json` enumerates six values.
55	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-beads.md` line 65 still documents
56	  three values (`open, in_progress, closed`). FEAT-004 lags the schema.
57	- `ddx bead list --json | jq -r '.[].status' | sort -u` on real projects
58	  returns only `open`, `in_progress`, `closed` for active rows today —
59	  `blocked`, `proposed`, and `cancelled` are schema-allowed but unused in
60	  the current active queue.
61	- The names `done`, `needs_human`, `pending`, `ready`, `review`,
62	  `needs_investigation` appear in code paths but are **not** persisted bead
63	  statuses today. Each falls into one of the other categories defined below.
64	
65	## Sections
66	
67	The remainder of this TD is structured as the eleven design sections required
68	by the originating bead, followed by five per-hygiene-bead contract
69	subsections.
70	
71	---
72	
73	## 1. Category Taxonomy
74	
75	Every state-machine name observed in DDx falls into exactly one of the
76	following categories. The category determines where the name is allowed to
77	live (schema, label namespace, event kind, queue derivation, or worker
78	process state).
79	
80	| Category | Storage location | Lifecycle | Owner |
81	|---|---|---|---|
82	| Persisted bead status | `status` field on the bead record | Mutated by atomic snapshot rewrite | Bead store, locked to bd/br set |
83	| Derived queue category | Computed on read from status + labels + deps | Never persisted | Queue derivation code (`ddx bead ready/blocked/status`) |
84	| Event kind | Append-only entry in `Extra["events"][].kind` | Append-only | Drain loop, agent service, CLI |
85	| Terminal phase | A persisted `closed` status plus a closing event/label that names *why* | Mutated once on close | Drain loop / CLI |
86	| Claim metadata | `assignee`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid` fields (preserved extras) | Set on claim, cleared on unclaim, expired by triage | Claim resolution path (TD-004) |
87	| Label | Entry in the `labels` array | Add/remove during normal mutation | Anyone with `ddx bead update` |
88	| Extra metadata field | Arbitrary key under preserved extras | Free-form per consumer | Subsystem owning that key |
89	| Worker state | In-memory state of the drain process | Lives only for the worker's lifetime | Drain loop process |
90	
91	A name MUST NOT span categories. If a name today appears as both a label and
92	a derived queue category (e.g., `ready` is derived; `ready` MUST NOT also
93	appear as a label), the sibling reconciliation bead removes the duplicate
94	usage.
95	
96	## 2. Persisted Status Enumeration
97	
98	The persisted bead status is exactly:
99	
100	```
101	open | in_progress | closed | blocked | proposed | cancelled
102	```
103	
104	This is the bd/br-canonical set; it matches `bead-record.schema.json`. This
105	TD does **not** add, rename, or remove any persisted status.
106	
107	Plain-English semantics:
108	
109	- `open` — the bead exists and is eligible for queue derivation. No claim is
110	  active.
111	- `in_progress` — the bead has an active claim (`assignee`, `claimed-at`,
112	  `claimed-pid` populated). The drain loop or an operator has taken
113	  ownership.
114	- `closed` — the bead is terminal. The reason for closure is encoded in a
115	  closing event and/or label; the status itself does not carry the reason.
116	- `blocked` — the bead cannot progress because of a hard precondition (an
117	  upstream dependency, an external blocker). The reason is encoded in a
118	  label (`blocked-on-upstream:<id>`) or an event.
119	- `proposed` — the bead is captured but has not been accepted into the
120	  active queue. Used for triage backlogs; not consumed by drain.
121	- `cancelled` — the bead was abandoned without completion. Distinct from
122	  `closed`: `cancelled` means "we are not doing this work"; `closed` means
123	  "the work is done (or determined unnecessary)".
124	
125	## 3. Transition Matrix
126	
127	| From → To | Allowed? | Driver | Event fired |
128	|---|---|---|---|
129	| `proposed` → `open` | yes | operator (`ddx bead update --status open`) | `triaged` |
130	| `proposed` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
131	| `open` → `in_progress` | yes | drain loop or operator (claim) | `claimed` |
132	| `open` → `blocked` | yes | operator or auto-triage | `blocked` |
133	| `open` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
134	| `in_progress` → `open` | yes | unclaim (operator or stale-claim sweep) | `unclaimed` |
135	| `in_progress` → `closed` | yes | drain loop (on merge/already-satisfied) or operator | `closed-merged` / `closed-already-satisfied` |
136	| `in_progress` → `blocked` | yes | drain loop on `review_block`, or operator | `blocked-review` / `blocked` |
137	| `blocked` → `open` | yes | operator (block resolved) | `unblocked` |
138	| `blocked` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
139	| `closed` → * | no | — | — (closed is terminal) |
140	| `cancelled` → * | no | — | — (cancelled is terminal) |
141	| any → `proposed` | no | — | proposed is an entry-only status |
142	
143	Closed and cancelled are terminal. Re-opening a closed bead is not a
144	transition; it is filing a follow-up bead with `replaces` set.
145	
146	## 4. Event Vocabulary (unified)
147	
148	Events are append-only entries on `Extra["events"]` (see TD-004 evidence
149	model). The kinds below form the closed vocabulary used across the five
150	hygiene beads. New kinds require updating this section.
151	
152	Lifecycle events:
153	
154	- `triaged` — `proposed → open`
155	- `claimed` — claim acquired
156	- `unclaimed` — claim released
157	- `blocked` — moved to `blocked` (carries a reason in `body`)
158	- `unblocked` — moved off `blocked`
159	- `closed-merged` — drain loop closed after a merge
160	- `closed-already-satisfied` — drain loop detected the work was already done
161	- `closed` — operator-driven close (catch-all)
162	- `cancelled` — moved to `cancelled`
163	
164	Drain-outcome events (no_changes family — NoChangesContract):
165	
166	- `no_changes_decomposed` — agent decomposed the bead instead of changing
167	  files
168	- `no_changes_blocked` — agent declared no_changes with a justified blocker
169	- `no_changes_no_evidence` — agent exited without commit and without a
170	  rationale file
171	- `no_changes_recoverable` — transient cause, will retry
172	
173	Drain-control events:
174	
175	- `drain-paused-quota` — QuotaPauseContract: drain paused because the active
176	  harness reported quota exhaustion
177	- `drain-resumed-quota`
178	- `rate-limit-retry` — RateLimitRetryContract: a single attempt was retried
179	  after a rate-limit response
180	- `lock-contention` — LockContentionContract: store-lock contention was
181	  observed and handled (retry or backoff)
182	
183	Review and triage events:
184	
185	- `review-block` — reviewer raised a BLOCKING finding
186	- `review-pass` — reviewer cleared the change
187	- `auto-triage` — TriageContract: triage path mutated labels/status
188	
189	Each event SHOULD include `kind`, `actor`, `created_at`, and a free-form
190	`body` (TD-004 schema). Drain events SHOULD additionally include the
191	`run-id` of the execution attempt in `body` or in a structured `extra`
192	field.
193	
194	## 5. Outcome → Label / Event / Extra Mapping
195	
196	When a drain attempt finishes, `execute-bead` returns one of a fixed set of
197	outcomes. The mapping below is normative.
198	
199	| Outcome | Status transition | Label changes | Events appended | Extra updates |
200	|---|---|---|---|---|
201	| `merged` | `in_progress → closed` | remove `claimed`, add `last-merged-rev:<sha>` (optional) | `closed-merged` | `Extra["last-run"]` updated with run-id |
202	| `already_satisfied` | `in_progress → closed` | (none) | `closed-already-satisfied` | `Extra["last-run"]` |
203	| `review_block` | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human` (label) | `review-block` | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref |
204	| `execution_failed` | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `unclaimed` + a structured failure event | `Extra["last-run"]` |
205	| `no_changes_decomposed` | `in_progress → closed` | add `decomposed` | `no_changes_decomposed` | `Extra["children"]` lists child IDs |
206	| `no_changes_blocked` | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human` | `no_changes_blocked` | `Extra["last-rationale"]` |
207	| `no_changes_no_evidence` | `in_progress → open` | add `triage` | `no_changes_no_evidence` | (none) |
208	| `no_changes_recoverable` | `in_progress → open` | (none) | `no_changes_recoverable` | (none) |
209	
210	`needs_human` is a **label**, not a status. It is the standard signal that
211	a human operator should look at the bead before drain re-attempts it.
212	
213	## 6. Claim Semantics
214	
215	Claim is metadata, not status. The persisted-status convention is that
216	`in_progress` implies a claim is held; `open` implies no claim. The actual
217	claim fields are:
218	
219	- `Extra["assignee"]` (string) — the entity holding the claim.
220	- `Extra["claimed-at"]` (RFC3339 timestamp) — when the claim was acquired.
221	- `Extra["claimed-pid"]` (int) — process id of the claim holder, advisory.
222	
223	Acquired:
224	
225	- During `open → in_progress` transition under the store lock.
226	- The drain loop and operator CLI (`ddx bead update --claim`) are the two
227	  legitimate drivers.
228	
229	Released:
230	
231	- Explicit `--unclaim` (or implicit unclaim on `in_progress → open`).
232	- On terminal close (`closed` / `cancelled`) the claim metadata is left in
233	  place as historical record but is no longer authoritative.
234	
235	Stale claim handling (TriageContract owns the policy):
236	
237	- A claim is *stale* if `claimed-at` is older than the configured stale
238	  threshold AND the bead is still `in_progress`.
239	- Stale claims are released by the auto-triage path (`auto-triage` event),
240	  status moves `in_progress → open`, and the bead becomes queue-eligible
241	  again.
242	- Auto-triage MUST NOT delete prior claim metadata; it appends an event
243	  recording the release.
244	
245	## 7. Naming-Role Decision Matrix
246	
247	Every name observed in code, schema, docs, or persisted data is assigned
248	a single category here. Names not in the persisted-status set MUST NOT
249	appear as `status` values.
250	
251	| Name | Category | Rationale |
252	|---|---|---|
253	| `open` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; queue-eligible default. |
254	| `in_progress` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; implies an active claim. |
255	| `closed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal-success path. |
256	| `blocked` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; hard precondition unmet. |
257	| `proposed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; pre-triage backlog. |
258	| `cancelled` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal-abandoned path. |
259	| `done` | Removed alias | Historical alias of `closed` in code paths. Not a persisted status. The reconciliation sibling bead replaces remaining code references with `closed`. |
260	| `pending` | Derived queue category | Used by queue derivation to mean "open AND has unmet deps" (a synonym for unsatisfied dependencies). Never persisted. The reconciliation sibling bead either renames it to `waiting` or folds it into `blocked` derivation. |
261	| `ready` | Derived queue category | "open AND no unmet deps AND not claimed". Computed from status+deps+claim; never persisted. |
262	| `review` | Terminal phase / event | A *phase of work*, not a status. Implemented as the `review-block` / `review-pass` event pair plus the `needs_human` label. Never persisted as a status. |
263	| `needs_human` | Label | Signals "an operator must intervene". Stays a label per bd/br compatibility — never a status. |
264	| `needs_investigation` | Label | Signals "the cause is unclear; triage required". Stays a **label** per bd/br compatibility. The reconciliation sibling bead removes any code path that treats it as a status. |
265	| `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` | Label | Parameterized label naming the upstream blocker. Distinct from the `blocked` status: a bead can carry the label while remaining `open` for visibility, or while `blocked` for enforcement. |
266	| `decomposed` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_decomposed`; pairs with `Extra["children"]`. |
267	| `triage` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_no_evidence` or by auto-triage. |
268	| `idle` / `draining` / `paused-quota` / `paused-rate-limit` / `exiting` | Worker state | Lives in the drain loop process, not on the bead. See section 10. |
269	
270	## 8. Per-Hygiene-Bead Contracts
271	
272	Each hygiene bead's AC must cite this section. Anything not in the
273	contract here is out of scope for that bead.
274	
275	### 8.1 NoChangesContract (ddx-b24e9630)
276	
277	Status transitions used:
278	
279	- `in_progress → closed` for `no_changes_decomposed` (terminal: children
280	  filed).
281	- `in_progress → blocked` for `no_changes_blocked` (operator follow-up
282	  expected).
283	- `in_progress → open` for `no_changes_no_evidence` and
284	  `no_changes_recoverable` (claim released, bead returns to the queue).
285	
286	Labels added:
287	
288	- `decomposed` (on `no_changes_decomposed`).
289	- `needs_human` (on `no_changes_blocked`).
290	- `triage` (on `no_changes_no_evidence`).
291	
292	Labels removed: none.
293	
294	Events fired: one of the four `no_changes_*` event kinds from section 4.
295	
296	Claim behavior: the claim is released on every outcome except
297	`no_changes_decomposed` and `no_changes_blocked`, where the bead leaves
298	`in_progress` directly to a terminal/blocked status.
299	
300	Loop interaction: the drain loop reads the outcome, applies the mapping
301	in section 5, and appends the matching event. It does not invent new
302	event kinds.
303	
304	### 8.2 TriageContract (ddx-3c154349)
305	
306	Status transitions used:
307	
308	- `in_progress → open` when releasing stale claims.
309	- `open → blocked` when triage decides a bead has an unmet hard
310	  precondition.
311	
312	Labels added: `triage`, `needs_human`, `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` (as
313	applicable).
314	
315	Labels removed: `triage` after a triaged bead is reclaimed cleanly (the
316	reverse mutation of `triage` is "next successful claim removes the
317	label").
318	
319	Events fired: `auto-triage`, `unclaimed`, optionally `blocked`.
320	
321	Claim behavior: the auto-triage sweep is the only path that releases a
322	claim it does not own. It MUST log the prior `assignee` and `claimed-at`
323	in the appended event body.
324	
325	Loop interaction: triage runs out-of-band of execute-bead. It MUST NOT
326	race with an active drain attempt holding the store lock — it acquires
327	the same store lock and releases-then-reclaims is not its job.
328	
329	### 8.3 QuotaPauseContract (ddx-aede917d)
330	
331	Status transitions used: none. Quota is a worker-state concern, not a
332	bead-state concern.
333	
334	Labels: none added on the affected bead. The current attempt is treated
335	as `execution_failed` (claim released, status returns to `open`).
336	
337	Events fired: `drain-paused-quota` is appended to a worker-scoped event
338	log (drain-process record), not to the bead's event stream. A
339	`unclaimed` event is appended to the bead.
340	
341	Claim behavior: the claim on the in-flight bead is released cleanly so
342	another worker (or a later resumed worker) can pick it up.
343	
344	Loop interaction: the worker transitions to worker-state
345	`paused-quota` (section 10). It stops claiming new beads until the
346	configured backoff elapses or an explicit resume signal arrives.
347	
348	### 8.4 RateLimitRetryContract (ddx-c6e3db02)
349	
350	Status transitions used: none. Rate-limit retry is internal to a single
351	attempt; the bead remains `in_progress` throughout.
352	
353	Labels: none.
354	
355	Events fired: `rate-limit-retry` per retry, on the bead's event stream.
356	Each event carries the retry count and the wait duration in the body.
357	
358	Claim behavior: claim is held continuously across retries.
359	
360	Loop interaction: rate-limit handling is bounded by the retry budget; on
361	budget exhaustion the outcome becomes `execution_failed` and the
362	standard mapping in section 5 applies. The worker briefly enters
363	`paused-rate-limit` for the wait window, then returns to `draining`.
364	
365	### 8.5 LockContentionContract (ddx-da11a34a)
366	
367	Status transitions used: none. Lock contention is a store-access
368	concern, not a bead-state concern.
369	
370	Labels: none.
371	
372	Events fired: `lock-contention` (append-only, on the affected bead, only
373	after the contention has been resolved and the mutation succeeded — so a
374	future audit can see that contention was observed). Pure read-side
375	contention does not emit an event.
376	
377	Claim behavior: unaffected. The claim is acquired or not; partial states
378	are not persisted.
379	
380	Loop interaction: the worker retries the locked operation with
381	exponential backoff up to a bounded budget. On budget exhaustion the
382	calling outcome maps to `execution_failed` (section 5). The worker does
383	not enter a dedicated worker-state for lock contention; it remains
384	`draining` and treats the failure as ordinary.
385	
386	## 9. Future-Change Process
387	
388	This is the rule of record for any subsequent change to bead state.
389	
390	> Any bead that introduces or changes a label, an event kind, an outcome →
391	> state mapping, claim handling, or worker-state semantics MUST cite the
392	> TD-031 section that authorizes the change. If no section authorizes it,
393	> TD-031 is amended in the same PR (or a parent bead) before the dependent
394	> work lands.
395	>
396	> Adding a new persisted bead status is **not** authorized by TD-031.
397	> It requires upstream bd/br coordination AND an amendment to ADR-004.
398	
399	In practice:
400	
401	- A new event kind: amend section 4 in the same PR.
402	- A new outcome → state mapping: amend section 5.
403	- A new label: amend section 7.
404	- A new worker state: amend section 10.
405	- A new persisted status: do not start the work; file an ADR-004
406	  amendment first.
407	
408	CI guard: a sibling bead adds a check that any change to
409	`bead-record.schema.json` or to the persisted-status enum touches
410	ADR-004 and TD-031 in the same commit. That guard is out of scope for
411	this bead.
412	
413	## 10. Worker-State Enumeration
414	
415	Worker state is the in-process state of the drain loop. It is **distinct
416	from bead state** and is not persisted in the bead store. It exists only
417	for the worker's lifetime.
418	
419	| Worker state | Meaning | Entry | Exit |
420	|---|---|---|---|
421	| `idle` | Worker is up but not actively claiming. | Worker startup; transient between attempts. | Operator or scheduler moves it to `draining`. |
422	| `draining` | Worker is actively claiming and executing beads. | Default operating state. | Quota or rate-limit pause; explicit stop. |
423	| `paused-quota` | Worker has observed quota exhaustion on its harness. | `drain-paused-quota` event. | `drain-resumed-quota` event after backoff or operator resume. |
424	| `paused-rate-limit` | Worker is sleeping out a rate-limit retry window inside an attempt. | Rate-limit response from harness. | Wait window elapses; worker resumes the same attempt. |
425	| `exiting` | Worker is shutting down cleanly; will not claim more beads. | Operator stop or terminal failure. | Process exit. |
426	
427	Worker state is observable via the worker's stdout/log; it is not stored
428	on any bead. Hygiene beads that affect worker state (QuotaPauseContract,
429	RateLimitRetryContract) MUST NOT add new fields to the bead schema to
430	represent these transient states.
431	
432	## 11. Migration Plan (placeholder)
433	
434	The actual migration survey — which existing beads carry orphaned status
435	values, which code paths persist non-canonical statuses, and what the
436	mechanical rename looks like — runs in the sibling reconciliation bead.
437	This TD does not catalogue current data.
438	
439	Sibling beads handling the migration:
440	
441	- Schema/docs/code reconciliation: align FEAT-004 line 65 with the
442	  schema; rename code references to non-status names; add CI guards.
443	- Hygiene-bead AC substitution: replace each hygiene bead's
444	  contract section with a reference to TD-031 §8.x.
445	- Migration survey: scan persisted bead stores in known DDx projects for
446	  non-canonical status values and produce a remediation list.
447	
448	When those siblings land, this section is replaced with a concrete
449	migration record citing the sibling bead IDs and the survey result.

9	// Bead represents a portable work item with metadata.
10	// The schema matches bd/br JSONL format for interchange compatibility.
11	// Unknown fields from external sources are preserved in Extra.
12	type Bead struct {
13		ID        string    `json:"id"`
14		Title     string    `json:"title"`
15		Status    string    `json:"status"`
16		Priority  int       `json:"priority"`
17		IssueType string    `json:"issue_type"`
18		Owner     string    `json:"owner,omitempty"`
19		CreatedAt time.Time `json:"created_at"`
20		CreatedBy string    `json:"created_by,omitempty"`
21		UpdatedAt time.Time `json:"updated_at"`
22	
23		// Optional fields (bd-compatible)
24		Labels      []string `json:"labels,omitempty"`
25		Parent      string   `json:"parent,omitempty"`
26		Description string   `json:"description,omitempty"`
27		Acceptance  string   `json:"acceptance,omitempty"`
28		Notes       string   `json:"notes,omitempty"`
29	
30		// Dependencies use bd-compatible format
31		Dependencies []Dependency `json:"dependencies,omitempty"`
32	
33		// Extra holds unknown fields for round-trip preservation.
34		// Workflow-specific fields (e.g. HELIX spec-id, execution-eligible)
35		// are stored here and written back on save.
36		Extra map[string]any `json:"-"`
37	}
38	
39	// Dependency represents a link between two beads (bd-compatible format).
40	type Dependency struct {
41		IssueID     string `json:"issue_id"`
42		DependsOnID string `json:"depends_on_id"`
43		Type        string `json:"type"` // "blocks", "related", etc.
44		CreatedAt   string `json:"created_at,omitempty"`
45		CreatedBy   string `json:"created_by,omitempty"`
46		Metadata    string `json:"metadata,omitempty"`
47	}

51		Kind      string    `json:"kind"`
52		Summary   string    `json:"summary,omitempty"`
53		Body      string    `json:"body,omitempty"`
54		Actor     string    `json:"actor,omitempty"`
55		CreatedAt time.Time `json:"created_at"`
56		Source    string    `json:"source,omitempty"`

59	// Status constants
60	const (
61		StatusOpen       = "open"
62		StatusInProgress = "in_progress"
63		StatusClosed     = "closed"
64		// StatusBlocked marks a bead that is not eligible for agent dispatch —
65		// either decomposed into child beads by the triage gate or parked for
66		// human intervention after a triage-overflow event. Blocked beads are
67		// excluded from ReadyExecution and will not be auto-dispatched.
68		StatusBlocked = "blocked"
69		// StatusProposed marks an operator-prompt bead awaiting approval. It is
70		// excluded from execute-loop drain until transitioned to open via the
71		// approval flow, or to cancelled when the operator declines it.
72		StatusProposed = "proposed"
73		// StatusCancelled marks a bead that was rejected before any execution
74		// took place (e.g. an operator-prompt bead the user chose not to run).
75		StatusCancelled = "cancelled"
76	)
77	
78	// IssueType constants
79	const (
80		// IssueTypeOperatorPrompt is the bead type used for operator-submitted
81		// prompts that the execute-loop runs as instructions.
82		IssueTypeOperatorPrompt = "operator-prompt"
83	)
84	
85	// Default labels and other defaults for operator-prompt beads.
86	const (
87		OperatorPromptLabelKind   = "kind:operator-prompt"
88		OperatorPromptLabelSource = "source:web-ui"
89		// OperatorPromptDefaultAcceptance is the auto-AC stub used when an
90		// operator submits a prompt without explicit acceptance criteria. The
91		// structural AC verifier is skipped for operator-prompt beads, so this
92		// stub stands as a human-readable contract only.
93		OperatorPromptDefaultAcceptance = "Agent must produce a diff or no_changes rationale; the prompt body is the contract."
94	)
95	
96	// IsValidStatusTransition reports whether a bead may move from `from` to `to`
97	// under the documented state machine. Self-transitions (from == to) and
98	// transitions to/from the empty string (used by Create defaults) are rejected;
99	// callers that want to seed a fresh status should set b.Status directly before
100	// validateBead runs.
101	//
102	// Allowed transitions:
103	//
104	//	open        → in_progress, closed, blocked
105	//	in_progress → open, closed, blocked
106	//	blocked     → open, closed
107	//	proposed    → open, cancelled
108	//	closed      → open                (reopen)
109	//	cancelled   → open                (reopen)
110	func IsValidStatusTransition(from, to string) bool {
111		if from == "" || to == "" || from == to {
112			return false
113		}
114		allowed := map[string]map[string]bool{
115			StatusOpen:       {StatusInProgress: true, StatusClosed: true, StatusBlocked: true},
116			StatusInProgress: {StatusOpen: true, StatusClosed: true, StatusBlocked: true},
117			StatusBlocked:    {StatusOpen: true, StatusClosed: true},
118			StatusProposed:   {StatusOpen: true, StatusCancelled: true},
119			StatusClosed:     {StatusOpen: true},
120			StatusCancelled:  {StatusOpen: true},
121		}
122		if next, ok := allowed[from]; ok {
123			return next[to]
124		}
125		return false
126	}
127	
128	// OperatorPromptMutationGuard enforces the no-self-mutation rule from
129	// Story 15: an operator-prompt bead's execution may not create, edit, or
130	// close another operator-prompt bead. The guard returns nil when the
131	// mutation is allowed and a non-nil error when it must be rejected.
132	//
133	// actorIssueType is the issue_type of the bead currently being executed
134	// (empty when no operator-prompt context is active). targetIssueType is
135	// the issue_type of the bead about to be mutated.
136	//
137	// Allow/deny matrix:
138	//
139	//	actor=""               , target=*                → allow
140	//	actor="task"           , target=*                → allow
141	//	actor="operator-prompt", target!="operator-prompt" → allow
142	//	actor="operator-prompt", target=="operator-prompt" → deny
143	func OperatorPromptMutationGuard(actorIssueType, targetIssueType string) error {
144		if actorIssueType != IssueTypeOperatorPrompt {
145			return nil
146		}
147		if targetIssueType != IssueTypeOperatorPrompt {
148			return nil
149		}
150		return fmt.Errorf("bead: operator-prompt bead may not mutate another operator-prompt bead")
151	}
152	
153	// NewOperatorPromptBead constructs a fresh operator-prompt bead from a raw
154	// prompt string, applying the Story 15 template defaults: title is the first
155	// non-empty line of the prompt, full prompt body is preserved verbatim in the
156	// description, default labels are kind:operator-prompt + source:web-ui, the
157	// status starts in `proposed` (approval flow), priority defaults to the
158	// caller-supplied tier (clamped to MinPriority..MaxPriority), and the
159	// acceptance field carries the auto-AC stub.
160	//
161	// The returned bead is not persisted; callers feed it to Store.Create which
162	// will assign the ID and CreatedAt/UpdatedAt timestamps.
163	func NewOperatorPromptBead(prompt string, defaultTier int) *Bead {
164		body := strings.TrimSpace(prompt)
165		title := body
166		if i := strings.IndexByte(body, '\n'); i >= 0 {
167			title = strings.TrimSpace(body[:i])
168		}
169		if title == "" {
170			title = "(empty operator prompt)"
171		}
172		tier := defaultTier
173		if tier < MinPriority {
174			tier = MinPriority
175		}
176		if tier > MaxPriority {
177			tier = MaxPriority
178		}
179		return &Bead{
180			Title:       title,
181			IssueType:   IssueTypeOperatorPrompt,
182			Status:      StatusProposed,
183			Priority:    tier,
184			Labels:      []string{OperatorPromptLabelKind, OperatorPromptLabelSource},
185			Description: body,
186			Acceptance:  OperatorPromptDefaultAcceptance,
187		}
188	}
189	
190	// Default values
191	const (
192		DefaultType     = "task"
193		DefaultStatus   = StatusOpen
194		DefaultPriority = 2
195		DefaultPrefix   = "bx" // used only when repo name detection fails
196		MinPriority     = 0
197		MaxPriority     = 4
198	)

200	// StatusCounts holds aggregate counts for a bead store.
201	type StatusCounts struct {
202		Open    int `json:"open"`
203		Closed  int `json:"closed"`
204		Blocked int `json:"blocked"`
205		Ready   int `json:"ready"`
206		Total   int `json:"total"`
207	}
208	
209	// Blocker kinds surfaced through BlockedAll. These strings are part of the
210	// external DDx contract (HELIX reads them to decide how to handle a blocker).
211	const (
212		BlockerKindDependency    = "dependency"
213		BlockerKindRetryCooldown = "retry-cooldown"
214	)
215	
216	// Blocker describes why an open bead is currently not runnable. Either
217	// unclosed dependencies exist, or an execute-loop cooldown has parked the
218	// bead until NextEligibleAt.
219	type Blocker struct {
220		Kind           string   `json:"kind"`
221		NextEligibleAt string   `json:"next_eligible_at,omitempty"`
222		UnclosedDepIDs []string `json:"unclosed_dep_ids,omitempty"`
223		LastStatus     string   `json:"last_status,omitempty"`
224		LastDetail     string   `json:"last_detail,omitempty"`
225	}
226	
227	// BlockedBead pairs a bead with its blocker classification.
228	type BlockedBead struct {
229		Bead
230		Blocker Blocker `json:"blocker"`
231	}

233	// DepIDs returns a flat list of dependency IDs for this bead.
234	func (b *Bead) DepIDs() []string {
235		var ids []string
236		for _, d := range b.Dependencies {
237			ids = append(ids, d.DependsOnID)
238		}
239		return ids
240	}
241	
242	// HasDep returns true if the bead depends on the given ID.
243	func (b *Bead) HasDep(id string) bool {
244		for _, d := range b.Dependencies {
245			if d.DependsOnID == id {
246				return true
247			}
248		}
249		return false
250	}
251	
252	// AddDep adds a dependency if it doesn't already exist.
253	func (b *Bead) AddDep(depID, depType string) {
254		for _, d := range b.Dependencies {
255			if d.DependsOnID == depID {
256				return // already exists
257			}
258		}
259		b.Dependencies = append(b.Dependencies, Dependency{
260			IssueID:     b.ID,
261			DependsOnID: depID,
262			Type:        depType,
263			CreatedAt:   time.Now().UTC().Format(time.RFC3339),
264		})
265	}
266	
267	// RemoveDep removes a dependency by target ID.
268	func (b *Bead) RemoveDep(depID string) {
269		var filtered []Dependency
270		for _, d := range b.Dependencies {
271			if d.DependsOnID != depID {
272				filtered = append(filtered, d)
273			}
274		}
275		b.Dependencies = filtered
276	}

9	# Solution Design: Beads Tracker
10	
11	## Overview
12	
13	This design specifies the runtime behavior of `ddx bead` and the file-backed
14	tracker used by HELIX and other workflows. The key design constraint is that
15	the default active-work collection must remain safe under concurrent access and
16	recoverable after partial corruption without requiring a separate database.
17	
18	## Goals
19	
20	- Keep the default backend human-readable and portable.
21	- Preserve unknown fields end-to-end for workflow-specific metadata.
22	- Make queue operations deterministic from a single parsed snapshot.
23	- Prevent corruption from concurrent writers.
24	- Self-heal partially corrupted JSONL when valid beads still exist.
25	- Expose enough context to diagnose malformed input without losing the whole queue.
26	
27	## Reuse Boundary
28	
29	The bead store is the reusable DDx record-store substrate, but the current
30	implementation is too tightly coupled to the primary tracker file.
31	
32	- The backend interface is bead-shaped, which is useful: it gives DDx one
33	  portable record schema with unknown-field preservation and interchangeable
34	  backends.
35	- The missing boundary is between the storage engine and one specific file such
36	  as `beads.jsonl`.
37	- DDx should support multiple named bead-backed collections, each with its own
38	  retention and indexing behavior.
39	
40	The reusable pieces are lower-level:
41	
42	- directory locking
43	- atomic temp-file swap
44	- repo-local backend selection conventions
45	- best-effort repair behavior where a line-oriented store is appropriate
46	- a stable bead-schema envelope with domain-specific fields in preserved extras
47	
48	Execution, metric, archive, and agent-session storage should reuse those
49	mechanics through separate bead-backed collections rather than sharing the
50	primary active-work file.

50	primary active-work file.
51	
52	## Data Format
53	
54	### Primary File
55	
56	- Path: `.ddx/beads.jsonl` for the default active-work collection
57	- Format: one JSON object per line
58	- Ordering: written sorted by `id`
59	- Semantics: each line is one full bead record; blank lines are ignored
60	
61	### Repair Artifacts
62	
63	- Backup path: `.ddx/beads.jsonl.bak`
64	- Temporary write path: `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`
65	- Backups are created only when the store auto-repairs a partially corrupted file
66	- The replacement write is atomic on the same filesystem via `rename`
67	
68	### Field Preservation
69	
70	Known fields are parsed into the bead struct. Unknown fields are preserved in
71	`Extra` and round-trip through read/write and import/export flows. This is the
72	mechanism that allows HELIX to store fields such as `spec-id`,
73	`execution-eligible`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid`, `superseded-by`, and
74	`replaces`.
75	
76	The design also reserves two workflow-facing shapes:
77	
78	- `assignee` is the advisory owner recorded by claim operations.
79	- `events` is an append-only array of execution evidence records stored in
80	  `Extra["events"]`.
81	
82	Each evidence record carries `kind`, `summary`, `body`, `actor`,
83	`created_at`, and `source`. DDx treats the array as ordered history and never
84	rewrites or compacts it during normal operations.
85	
86	## Claim Algorithm
87	
88	Claim/unclaim stays a normal bead mutation, but ownership is now explicit.
89	
90	1. Acquire the bead store lock.
91	2. Read the current bead snapshot.
92	3. Resolve the assignee from the explicit `--assignee` flag, then runtime
93	   caller identity, then `ddx` as the fallback.
94	4. For `--claim`, set `status=in_progress`, `assignee`, `claimed-at`, and
95	   `claimed-pid`.
96	5. For `--unclaim`, set `status=open` and clear `assignee`, `claimed-at`, and
97	   `claimed-pid`.
98	6. Serialize the full snapshot to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`.
99	7. Atomically rename the temp file into place.
100	
101	Claims remain advisory; the store does not introduce a hard reservation lock.
102	The metadata exists so humans and agents can distinguish who holds the claim
103	and when it happened.
104	
105	## Evidence Algorithm
106	
107	Execution evidence is append-only.
108	
109	1. Acquire the bead store lock.
110	2. Read the current bead snapshot.
111	3. Load the target bead and append one evidence entry to `Extra["events"]`.
112	4. Populate `kind`, `summary`, `body`, `actor`, `created_at`, and `source`.
113	5. Serialize the full snapshot to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`.
114	6. Atomically rename the temp file into place.
115	
116	This deliberately preserves full history. Evidence writes never mutate or
117	remove existing entries, so close summaries and experiment outcomes remain
118	auditable.
119	
120	## Read Path
121	
122	The read path is intentionally best-effort.
123	
124	1. Read the full file from disk.
125	2. Scan line-by-line instead of parsing the entire file as one JSON value.
126	3. Trim whitespace per line.
127	4. Unmarshal each non-empty line independently.
128	5. Preserve valid beads in a snapshot.
129	6. Emit line-numbered warnings for malformed records.
130	7. Return `events` history and claim metadata exactly as stored.
131	8. If at least one valid bead exists and at least one malformed record was seen,
132	   trigger repair under the store lock.
133	9. If no valid bead exists, return a contextual error that names the file and
134	   malformed-record count.
135	
136	Why this shape:
137	
138	- A single malformed record should not break `ready`, `blocked`, `status`, or
139	  `list`.
140	- Line numbers are enough to isolate the bad record quickly.
141	- A full snapshot keeps queue views deterministic within a single command.
142	
143	## Repair Path
144	
145	Repair only runs when the file contains a mix of valid and malformed records.
146	
147	1. Acquire the bead store lock.
148	2. Re-read the current file under the lock.
149	3. Reparse it to verify it still needs repair.
150	4. Copy the current file to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.bak` using a temp file plus rename.
151	5. Rewrite the cleaned bead snapshot using the normal atomic writer.
152	6. Leave the backup in place for inspection and rollback.
153	
154	This prevents concurrent readers from racing to repair the same file after it
155	has already been fixed by another process.
156	
157	## Write Path
158	
159	Mutating commands use the same pattern:
160	
161	1. Acquire the store lock.
162	2. Read the current bead snapshot.
163	3. Apply the mutation in memory.
164	4. Validate the result.
165	5. Serialize the full snapshot to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`.
166	6. Rename the temp file to `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
167	
168	This ensures there is never a partially written main tracker file. The lock
169	serializes writers, and the temp-file swap prevents readers from seeing a half
170	written JSONL record.
171	
172	## Queue Derivation
173	
174	The tracker queue views are derived from one in-memory snapshot.
175	
176	### Ready
177	
178	- Consider only `open` beads.
179	- A bead is ready when every dependency is `closed`.
180	- Execution-ready views additionally filter on `execution-eligible` and
181	  `superseded-by`.
182	- Results are sorted by priority, then by stable iteration order from the
183	  parsed snapshot.
184	
185	### Blocked
186	
187	- Consider only `open` beads.
188	- A bead is blocked when at least one dependency is not `closed`.
189	
190	### Status
191	
192	- `Total` is the number of parsed beads.
193	- `Open`, `Closed`, `Ready`, and `Blocked` are derived from the same snapshot.
194	- Status reporting never reparses the file independently of the queue view.
195	- Evidence history stored in `Extra["events"]` does not affect queue derivation.
196	- Claim metadata does not affect queue derivation beyond the bead's status.
197	
198	## Concurrency Model
199	
200	- Directory locks prevent simultaneous writes.
201	- Reads are allowed without the lock, but the repair path uses the lock before
202	  rewriting anything.
203	- No API writes directly to `beads.jsonl`; all mutation goes through `WriteAll`
204	  or a higher-level store method that calls it.
205	- External tools are expected to use `ddx bead`, not edit the JSONL directly.
206	
207	This model is sufficient for the expected HELIX/DDX usage pattern:
208	multiple agents can race to claim or update work, but the store will serialize
209	the resulting file writes and either preserve or repair the tracker snapshot.
210	
211	## Performance Targets
212	
213	The feature spec sets the target at under 100 ms for local operations on up to
214	10,000 beads. The design supports that target by:
215	
216	- using a single sequential parse of the JSONL file
217	- avoiding database overhead
218	- deriving queue views from one snapshot
219	- keeping writes as a single temp-file pass plus rename
220	
221	## Validation Matrix
222	
223	These are the key tests that validate the design:
224	
225	- `go test ./internal/bead/...`
226	- `go test ./cmd -run 'TestBead' -count=1`
227	- `TestConcurrentCreatesSerialized`
228	- `TestMalformedJSONLSkipsBadRecords`
229	- `TestMalformedJSONLAllBadReturnsError`
230	- `TestBeadClaimUsesExplicitAssignee`
231	- `TestBeadClaimFallsBackToCallerIdentity`
232	- `TestBeadUnclaimClearsClaimMetadata`
233	- `TestBeadEvidenceAppendPreservesOrder`
234	- `TestBeadEvidenceAppendAtomicWithConcurrentWriters`
235	- `TestBeadShowJSONIncludesEvidenceHistory`
236	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`
237	
238	Expected outcomes:
239	
240	- Concurrent creates complete without corruption.
241	- A mixed-validity JSONL file is repaired with a `.bak` backup.
242	- An all-malformed JSONL file fails with a contextual error.
243	- Queue commands keep working when at least one valid record remains.
244	
245	## Failure Modes
246	
247	- If the file is completely malformed, the command fails instead of fabricating
248	  empty state.
249	- If the repair backup cannot be written, the read returns an error and leaves
250	  the original file untouched.
251	- If a lock cannot be acquired in time, the mutating command fails cleanly.
252	- If evidence append fails, the mutation fails atomically and the prior bead
253	  snapshot remains unchanged.
254	
255	## Non-Goals
256	
257	- Real-time sync between multiple bead stores.
258	- Database-backed mutation logic in the default backend.
259	- Workflow-specific validation policy in the bead engine itself.
260	
261	Those concerns belong in higher layers such as HELIX hooks, import/export, or
262	alternative backends.

input.new_string:
## Overview

This design specifies the runtime behavior of `ddx bead` and the file-backed
tracker used by HELIX and other workflows. The key design constraint is that
the default active-work collection must remain safe under concurrent access and
recoverable after partial corruption without requiring a separate database.

The persisted bead `status` enum, the allowed status transitions, the event
vocabulary appended to `Extra["events"]`, the claim-metadata semantics, and
the worker-state taxonomy are normatively defined in
[TD-031: Bead State Machine and Naming-Role Decisions](../technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md).
This design references TD-031 rather than restating those decisions; any
divergence between this file and TD-031 is a bug in this file.
